title: WebP CVE writeup — controlling the write (part 2, GetNextKey reversal)
html:
Continuation of the libwebp Huffman writeup — explains the HuffmanCode struct (1-byte bits, 2-byte value, 4 bytes total), shows the attacker-controllable 2-byte payload window, and walks through the GetNextKey bit-reversal that determines write index. Strategy: build four 15-bit codes so the 4th HuffmanCode lands at index 0x60 (overlapping the next heap chunk), then thread 9-bit codes to fine-tune the index in 2-byte increments.

TL;DR

  • HuffmanCode { uint8_t bits; uint16_t value } — 2-byte value is attacker-controllable (range 0..255 for RGB, 0..39 for distance channel).
  • GetNextKey writes by reverse(prefix), not sequentially — exploit calculates the index sequence under 15-bit prefix length.
  • 4× 15-bit codes drop the 4th write at 0x60, overlapping next-chunk metadata; 9-bit codes tune the index.
  • Continuation of the part 1 capture.
  • Same week as the May 19 IDA reverse engineering session on procps_scan.

webp cve · reverse engineering · buffer overflow · cs coursework
